Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to study, and sometimes I'd spend hours on the LMS watching videos or clicking through stuff, but it never felt organized. I'd usually just do whatever felt least overwhelming in the moment, which meant I jumped around a lot without any real plan. My attendance was pretty bad because I just couldn't make myself show up consistently.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd be really active online, watching videos and posting in discussions, then other weeks I'd barely touch anything. Deadlines would sometimes wake me up, but even then I'd often miss them or submit something half-finished. I wanted to be more consistent, but something always seemed to get in the way of actually following through.
Q3: Usually it was anxiety that got me to log in. I'd suddenly remember I had something due or feel this wave of panic about falling behind, and I'd rush to the LMS to see what I was missing. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in because they felt less intimidating than regular assignments. Other times I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I wasn't really accomplishing much.
Q4: For a while, the gamified elements made logging in feel almost automatic. I'd want to check my progress or see if I'd unlocked anything new, which was kind of addictive in a weird way. But that routine didn't last because the stress of actually completing real assignments overshadowed the fun parts. Eventually logging in started feeling more like a source of anxiety than a habit.
Q5: The videos were genuinely helpful when I could focus on them. I learn better when I can see things being done rather than just reading about them. The gamified tasks were also good because they broke things into smaller pieces that felt manageable. Discussion posts helped sometimes because seeing other students' questions made me feel less alone in being confused.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and longer written resources. I'd open them with good intentions but then my mind would wander and I'd close them without really absorbing anything. The dense academic texts just didn't work for how I learn. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually see myself making progress or if it had that game-like quality where you get immediate feedback. I needed to feel like I was moving forward, not just grinding through something abstract. If something felt too long or too complicated, my brain would just check out and I'd start feeling overwhelmed and anxious.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a relief because there was no pressure attached to them. I could explore them without the crushing weight of knowing I'd fail if I messed up. Required ones made my anxiety spike, which ironically made me avoid them even more. It's messed up, but the things that mattered most were often the ones I procrastinated on the hardest.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download videos and watch them on my phone when I was lying in bed or on the bus. I also used YouTube to find explanations of concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials. Sometimes I'd take handwritten notes because typing felt too disconnected. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer where the LMS couldn't track it.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be rewatching downloaded content or working through problems in a notebook, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also spend time just thinking through concepts while doing other things, like walking or trying to fall asleep. The LMS only saw when I clicked things, not when I was actually wrestling with ideas in my head.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once online, download it, then rewatch it multiple times offline because I needed repetition to understand things. The LMS would only show that single initial view, but I might have watched it three or four more times on my own. That's just how I learn, through repetition and hands-on practice that doesn't require being logged in.
Q12: There was a period mid-semester when everything just felt impossible. My anxiety was through the roof, I wasn't sleeping well, and every time I thought about the course I'd feel this wave of dread. I stopped attending classes almost entirely and my assignment completion dropped. I was still logging in and watching videos, but I couldn't translate that into actual submitted work.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or exhausted, I'd log into the LMS and just... scroll. I'd watch videos without really processing them, or I'd start an assignment and then abandon it halfway through. Competing responsibilities made it worse because I'd feel guilty about the course while doing other things, then guilty about other things while trying to study. It was this constant cycle of anxiety.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, videos playing, discussion threads in front of me, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd watched twenty minutes of a lecture without absorbing a single thing. It looked like engagement from the outside, but inside I was just going through the motions to ease my guilt.
Q15: All the time. The bigger and more important an assignment was, the more I'd avoid it. It's like my brain would freeze up when facing something that really mattered. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd find some excuse. The anxiety made it impossible to just sit down and begin, even when I knew I was making things worse.
Q16: Deadlines would create these panic spikes where I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS. I'd cram in video watching and try to rush through materials I should have engaged with weeks earlier. But by then it was usually too late to do quality work, so I'd either submit something mediocre or miss the deadline entirely. The deadline pressure never translated into good preparation.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it was a mixed bag. Positive feedback gave me a brief boost, but negative feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid that part of the course entirely. I think I needed more immediate, low-stakes feedback like what the gamified tasks provided. The delayed feedback on big assignments just added to my anxiety without helping me improve.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming because everything seemed to build on everything else. If I missed one thing, I felt like I was permanently behind, which made me want to disengage even more. The gamified elements helped break that feeling sometimes, but the overall course structure assumed a level of consistent engagement I just couldn't maintain. More flexibility would have helped me a lot.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt achievable and gave instant gratification. But the heavy reading requirements and the way assignments were weighted made things harder. When so much of my grade depended on things I'd already fallen behind on, it felt pointless to even try. The course design didn't really account for someone who was struggling to keep up.
Q20: That was literally me. I was active because activity felt productive and eased my anxiety temporarily. But submitting work meant facing judgment and potential failure, which terrified me. It's easier to watch videos and participate in discussions than to put yourself out there with an assignment that could confirm you're not good enough. Activity was my way of feeling involved without risking real failure.
Q21: I can see how that would happen if someone learns efficiently offline or already knows the material. That wasn't really my experience though. My low activity periods usually meant I was struggling, not that I was secretly mastering things elsewhere. But I know some people just need less hand-holding than I do and can perform well without constant LMS presence.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when and how often someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on things. But they can't show whether someone is actually understanding the material or just staring at it blankly. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, and most importantly, the emotional state behind the activity. My logs would show engagement, but they'd miss how anxious and lost I actually felt.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material and the course community. It's not just clicking through things or watching videos on autopilot. Real engagement is when you're curious, when you want to understand, when you feel like you're part of something. I wanted to feel that way, but my anxiety kept getting in the way of genuine connection.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally or how much anxiety was driving my behavior. It wouldn't show the hours I spent worrying about the course without actually studying, or the offline attempts to catch up that never quite worked. It also wouldn't show that despite failing, I actually cared a lot. I wasn't disengaged because I didn't care, I was disengaged because I was overwhelmed and didn't know how to cope.
